queries.validated_fix: Inspect and replace worn aileron hinge bolts, bushings, and bellcrank components. Remove the ailerons, inspect all hinge points for elongated holes and worn bushings. Replace as needed. After reassembly, verify aileron travel: UP = 20° ± 1°, DOWN = 15° ± 1° (verify for specific model). Check aileron cable tension per the rigging chart. NOTE: Aileron free play can cause adverse handling and flutter risk at higher speeds.

queries.query: The propeller hasn't been overhauled and the mechanic says it may be due based on calendar time.
queries.validated_fix: Inspect and overhaul the propeller per McCauley Service Bulletin and Lycoming/Cessna recommendations. The McCauley 1C235 fixed-pitch propeller on the 172I-M has no mandatory TBO, but McCauley recommends overhaul every 1,500-2,000 hours or 5-6 years of calendar time (whichever comes first). Overhaul includes: disassembly, non-destructive testing (NDT -- dye penetrant or eddy current), dimensional checks, blade dressing, and rebalancing. Done by a certified propeller repair station. NOTE: Calendar time limits are important -- propellers develop internal corrosion even if not flown. A propeller that has been on the aircraft for 20+ years without overhaul should be inspected/overhauled regardless of hour time.

queries.technical_issue: Main Gear Axle / Brake Assembly Corrosion
queries.query: The mechanic found corrosion on the main gear axle and brake caliper during the annual.
queries.validated_fix: Inspect, clean, and treat or replace corroded brake components. Cleveland brake assemblies (standard on the 172) are aluminum and steel -- both corrode. Clean corrosion per approved methods. Replace corroded axle hardware. Rebuild the brake caliper with new seals, pistons, and linings if corrosion has affected sealing surfaces. Also check brake disc minimum thickness and replace if below limits. NOTE: Brake system corrosion is accelerated by aircraft stored outdoors or on wet/grass surfaces. Apply ACF-50 corrosion preventive to non-friction surfaces during annual.

queries.query: The engine is at TBO hours and the mechanic says I need to decide whether to overhaul or keep monitoring.
queries.validated_fix: The Lycoming O-320-E2D TBO is 2,000 hours. For Part 91 (private) operations, TBO is a RECOMMENDATION, not a mandatory requirement -- the engine can continue to operate past TBO if it is maintained in an airworthy condition (good compression, oil consumption within limits, no metal in oil, all ADs complied with). However, engine failure risk increases past TBO. Options: (1) Major overhaul -- complete teardown, inspection, and rebuild to factory new limits. Cost: $25,000-35,000+ installed. (2) Top overhaul -- replace cylinders, pistons, rings, valves, and guides without splitting the crankcase. Less expensive but doesn't address crankshaft, camshaft, or bearing wear. (3) Factory remanufacture -- exchange for a zero-time factory remanufactured engine (Lycoming). (4) Continue operating with enhanced monitoring (frequent oil analysis, compression checks, borescope inspections). NOTE: Insurance companies may require overhaul at TBO. For Part 135 (commercial) operations, TBO IS mandatory. The decision to operate past TBO should be made with input from your A&P/IA and with full understanding of the risks.

queries.query: The cabin heat barely works -- it's cold in the cockpit during winter flying.
queries.validated_fix: Inspect the cabin heat system: (1) Check the heater valve and cable -- the valve on the muffler shroud must open fully. Adjust or replace the cable and valve. (2) Check the muffler shroud for leaks or gaps -- air bypasses the shroud and reduces heating. Seal or replace the shroud. (3) Check the heater ducting from the shroud to the cabin -- disconnected or collapsed ducts lose heat. (4) Check the exhaust muffler -- the heat comes from the muffler surface, and a corroded muffler may have reduced surface area. (5) Check the cabin air inlet -- if the fresh air inlet is restricted, the volume of air through the heater is reduced. NOTE: The Cessna 172's cabin heat system is entirely engine-exhaust-dependent. If the heat system is inadequate, verify the muffler integrity FIRST -- a cracked muffler not only reduces heat but introduces CO into the cabin. Never fly in icing conditions without adequate defroster capability.

queries.query: The cabin heat barely works -- it's cold in the cockpit during winter flying.
queries.validated_fix: Inspect the cabin heat system: (1) Check the heater valve and cable -- must open fully. (2) Check muffler shroud for leaks/gaps. (3) Check heater ducting for disconnections. (4) Check muffler integrity -- cracked muffler reduces heat AND introduces CO. (5) Check cabin air inlet for restriction. NOTE: Verify muffler integrity FIRST -- a cracked muffler is a CO hazard. Never fly in icing conditions without adequate defroster capability.

queries.query: The exhaust manifold studs are corroded and one broke off during removal -- now there's a stuck stud in the cylinder head.
queries.validated_fix: Extract the broken stud using a stud extractor (EZ-Out) after soaking with penetrating oil. If extraction fails, drill and re-tap or install a Heli-Coil thread insert per approved repair data. Replace all exhaust studs and brass/stainless lock nuts with new hardware during any exhaust service. Apply high-temperature anti-seize compound to new studs for future serviceability. NOTE: Exhaust fastener corrosion is universal on aircraft -- the heat cycling and combustion byproducts attack the threads. Always use anti-seize and replace hardware whenever the exhaust system is serviced.
